topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the graduate seminar room hummed with an irritating persistence. I stared at the whiteboard, the meticulously planned outline of my research dissolving into meaningless scribbles as I reread the final email. Dr. Albright was leaving. Gone. Poof. My entire scaffolding for the next six months had just been dismantled by a sudden administrative decree. I slammed my notebook shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the otherwise quiet room. My jaw tightened, and I felt a hot flush creep up my neck. I started pacing the narrow aisle between the desks, my footsteps sharp and uneven. "This is impossible," I muttered to the empty chair opposite me, the words tasting like ash. I picked up my pen, intending to start drafting a frantic appeal, but my hand trembled too much. I just sat down heavily, the chair protesting beneath my weight, and let the silence stretch, heavy and suffocating.

[story 2]

I kept smoothing down the wrinkles on my already perfectly ironed shirt, a nervous habit I’d developed over the last few weeks. Every time I glanced at the departmental bulletin board, my stomach performed a slow, unpleasant flip. Dr. Albright’s name was conspicuously absent from the upcoming faculty changes list. It was the final confirmation, delivered with the casual indifference of someone clearing out an old office. I leaned back in my chair, forcing a slow, deliberate breath in, trying to regulate the rapid thumping beneath my ribs. "Of course," I whispered, the sound thin and brittle. I picked up my laptop, the screen glaring back at me, and began deleting paragraphs, then retyping them, the process becoming a mindless, repetitive motion. My fingers hovered over the keys, resisting the urge to smash the keyboard, focusing instead on the mundane task of formatting citations, anything to avoid the gaping void where guidance used to reside.

[story 3]

The coffee in my mug had gone cold, mirroring the sudden drop in my internal temperature. I stood by the window, watching the autumn leaves skitter across the quad, feeling a profound, heavy weariness settle over my shoulders. Dr. Albright’s departure felt less like a professional transition and more like a personal betrayal. I clenched my fists until my knuckles were white, the tension radiating up my arms. I turned away from the view, focusing instead on the stack of annotated readings on my desk. Each page seemed to mock my efforts, the dense theoretical arguments suddenly feeling impenetrable without the specific lens Dr. Albright had provided. I sighed, a long, ragged sound that seemed to pull all the air from the room. I pushed the books away, the sudden movement sharp and jerky, and walked over to the filing cabinet, pulling out old notes just to feel something tangible, something solid, in this swirling uncertainty.
